one_line_takeaway: A methodology survey of RL for LLMs (PPO, DPO, GRPO, RLHF/RLAIF, RLVR) whose value-add is per-method mathematical failure-mode analysis and an Alignment-vs-Reasoning taxonomy; safety/reward-hacking themes appear throughout but it is a training-technique review, not a security study.
key_finding: The survey's synthesized conclusions: RLHF remains the dominant paradigm for subjective alignment but incurs an 'Alignment Tax' as the KL-regularized optimum p*(y|x) proportional to p_SFT(y|x)exp(r(x,y)/beta) drives mode-seeking entropy collapse; outcome-based and verifiable-reward RL (RLVR) markedly improve stepwise reasoning where correctness is programmatically checkable, but at the cost of sparse, high-variance binary gradients that treat 'almost correct' identically to complete hallucination. A recurring cross-cutting theme is that every learned reward or verifier is eventually gameable, so deterministic-oracle methods (RLVR) trade epistemic certainty for exploration difficulty, while learned-critic methods trade signal density for proxy-gaming risk. The authors emphasize distinguishing benchmark gains from architectural scaling versus those from the optimization objective itself.
core_contribution: A technical survey (ACM TIST, accepted June 2026) of reinforcement-learning techniques for large language models by Srivastava (U. Georgia) and Aggarwal (Purdue). Its distinguishing contribution over prior RL/LLM reviews is that each surveyed method is paired with two dedicated modules: an 'Algorithmic Analysis' that mathematically frames the method's structural bottlenecks (e.g., PPO's 'Four-Model Bottleneck' ~4N GPU-memory footprint; GRPO's O(1/sqrt(G)) advantage-noise and sigma->0 singularity in homogeneous-reward regimes; off-policy Q-learning's overestimation over a >50,000-token vocabulary), and a 'Critical Analysis' that derives why methods fail (RLHF's entropy-collapsing 'Alignment Tax'; DPO's unbounded log-ratio and length-bias exploitation; Constitutional AI's 'Critique-Revision Gap' and over-refusal; V-RL's 'Adversarial Goodharting'). It also proposes a two-branch hierarchical taxonomy separating Alignment methods (RLHF, RLAIF, Constitutional AI, DPO, UNA) from Reasoning methods (OB-RL, CoT-RO, Verifier-Guided RL, RLVR), organized by whether the optimization target is subjective desirability or objective validity.
threat_model: None. This is a survey of ML training methodology, not a security paper, so it defines no attacker, asset, or trust boundary. It does, however, describe several adversarial-flavored optimization dynamics as failure modes of the surveyed methods (not as a threat model the paper studies empirically): reward hacking / Goodhart's Law (the policy exploiting reward-function loopholes), 'Adversarial Goodharting' in Verifier-Guided RL (a sufficiently capable policy eventually breaks a fixed learned verifier by ascending its approximation-error manifold), proxy gaming and specification gaming against non-exhaustive unit-test verifiers in RLVR, sycophancy/model-collapse feedback loops in RLAIF from non-cancelling systematic bias delta(x), and red-teaming as a Constitutional-AI training component.
limitations: As a survey it contributes no new empirical evidence; its quantitative anchors (parameter counts, memory scaling, group-size heuristics, the ~50B Constitutional-AI phase-transition threshold) are drawn from or estimated relative to cited literature rather than measured here. The 'Critical Analysis' derivations are heuristic/asymptotic arguments rather than proven theorems.

quantitative_results: Model-scale progression cited to motivate alignment need (all from prior work, not measured here): GPT-2 1.5B, GPT-3 175B, PaLM 540B, LLaMA 3.1 405B, DeepSeek-V3 (Mixture-of-Experts) 671B parameters

PPO 'Four-Model Bottleneck': active memory ~= M_pi + M_ref + M_r + M_V, approaching 4N total GPU memory since value and reference models share the policy's parameter count N; FSDP sharding typically needed above 7B parameters
PPO clip parameter epsilon typically 0.1 or 0.2 (stated in the survey's PPO objective)
Off-policy Q-learning overestimation arises because the Bellman max is taken over a vocabulary of >50,000 tokens
GRPO: advantage noise scales as O(1/sqrt(G)); practitioners use group size G >= 64; standard-deviation normalization creates a sigma->0 singularity when all rewards are 0 or all are 1
Constitutional AI: a heuristic phase-transition threshold of ~50B parameters below which the self-revision operator is 'lossy' (degrades coherence)

DPO implicit reward r_hat = beta*log(p_theta(y|x)/p_ref(y|x)) is theoretically unbounded, versus a trained reward model constrained to e.g. [-3, 3], explaining susceptibility to length bias and noisy-label overfitting
RLVR reward is a binary indicator R in {0,1} (V_x(y)=True -> 1, else 0); TÜLU 3 is cited as using RLVR to reach state-of-the-art math results by verifying intermediate execution traces
GRPO originated in DeepSeekMath and was used to train DeepSeek-R1 (cited empirical performance, not reproduced here)
